FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract explicitly states "Dutch ABCD Study and the Spanish INMA Project" — not the U.S. ABCD Study. This is a European study using different cohorts.

notes: Paper uses Dutch ABCD and Spanish INMA cohorts, not U.S. ABCD. Excluded at FT-IC1.

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract and methods confirm "21-site adolescent brain cognitive development study" with baseline data from ABCD, U.S. representative sample, children aged 9-11 years.

FT_IC5.evidence: Cross-sectional empirical analysis using generalized linear mixed models (GLMMs) with reported coefficients for electronic media use categories and anxiety/depression outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Child Psychiatry & Human Development (2019).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 match: children self-reported electronic media use with specific phone/SM modalities (texting, social networking sites, video chat) paired with individual-trait outcomes (child anxiety and depression scales from CBCL). Paper reports estimates for specific EMU categories.

FT_IC3.evidence: GLMMs report coefficients and t-values for relationships between EMU categories and anxiety/depression (e.g., child reported weekday EMU with anxiety: t=3.309, p=0.001).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric quantitative results reported throughout (coefficients, t-values, 95% CIs, p-values) for SM/phone ↔ outcome relationships.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (baseline only), so FT-EC7 carve-out applies. Paper does not meet FT-EC7 criteria.
notes: Paper examines differential relationships of video gaming and video chatting to anxiety (more robust) vs. depression (weaker after controlling for anxiety). Includes separate child and parent reports.

FT_IC1.evidence: Methods explicitly state "Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study, a broadly US representative sample of 11,875 children aged 9 to 10 years" with data from 21 sites across the United States.

FT_IC5.evidence: Cross-sectional baseline empirical analysis (2016–18) using negative binomial structural equation modeling with reported incidence rate ratios (IRRs) and 95% CIs for ST types and problem behaviors.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity (2019).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 match: children self-reported screen time types including television/movies, videos, video games, and social media (texting, social networking sites, video chat)—phone/SM-specific items. Paired with individual-trait outcomes: problem behaviors measured by Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) across 8 syndromes (anxious/depressed, withdrawn/depressed, somatic complaints, social problems, thought problems, attention problems, rule-breaking, aggressive behavior).

FT_IC3.evidence: Negative binomial regression reports incidence rate ratios (IRRs) for all ST behaviors and problem behaviors (e.g., TV/movies with rule-breaking: IRR=1.059, 95% CI: 0.025, 0.090, p<.001; mature-rated games with somatic complaints: IRR=1.041, p=0.009).

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results reported throughout: IRRs, p-values, 95% CIs, and indirect effects (mediation) for all SM/phone ↔ outcome relationships. Also reports sleep duration mediation effects.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional baseline (2016–18), so FT-EC7 carve-out applies. Paper does not meet FT-EC7 criteria.
notes: Paper examines ST types (television/movies, videos, video games, social media) and content (mature-rated video games, R-rated movies) in relation to 8 CBCL problem behavior syndromes. Sleep duration mediates associations, though effect sizes are small. Large nationally representative sample (n=11,875).
